params.text:
A child in need vs a child at risk of significant harm

Not every worry means a child is being abused. The Children Act 1989 sets out two important thresholds that shape what help a child receives.

  • Child in need (section 17) – a child who is unlikely to reach or keep a reasonable standard of health or development without extra support, or who is disabled. These children need help, but the situation is not necessarily one of harm. Support is offered on a voluntary basis with the family.
  • Child at risk of significant harm (section 47) – where a local authority has reasonable cause to suspect a child is suffering, or is likely to suffer, significant harm, it has a duty to make enquiries. This triggers a child protection response.

There is no fixed legal definition of “significant” harm. Practitioners weigh up the severity, duration and frequency of the harm, and its impact on the child's health and development.

Thresholds and early help

Support for children works as a continuum. At the lowest level of need, universal services meet a child's needs. When extra support is needed, early help steps in – co-ordinated, voluntary support offered before problems become serious. Where needs are more complex, a child in need assessment under section 17 may follow, and where there is a risk of significant harm, a section 47 child protection enquiry is triggered.

Each local safeguarding partnership publishes a threshold document that describes the levels of need and when a case should be referred. If you are ever unsure which level a concern sits at, that is precisely when you should seek advice rather than decide alone.

params.question: A local authority has 'reasonable cause to suspect' that a child is suffering, or is likely to suffer, significant harm. Which duty does this trigger?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Section 47 of the Children Act 1989 places a duty on the local authority to make enquiries where there is reasonable cause to suspect significant harm.

answers.text: A section 17 child in need assessment only

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Section 17 covers children in need of support, but a suspicion of significant harm raises the response to a section 47 child protection enquiry.

answers.text: No formal duty; the family can ask for help if they wish

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Where significant harm is suspected, the local authority has a legal duty to act; it does not depend on the family requesting help.

answers.text: Automatic removal of the child from the home

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A section 47 enquiry is an investigation to decide what action is needed. It does not automatically mean a child is removed.
